howItWorks.intro: This heat stress calculator uses established formulas to provide accurate results.
rules: WBGT estimates wet-bulb globe temperature from temp + humidity + sun
Thresholds: Light work 86°F, Moderate 82°F, Heavy 77°F WBGT
OSHA recommends work/rest cycles when WBGT exceeds thresholds
howItWorks.outro: Results are estimates based on standard formulas. Verify with current local data for your specific situation.

faq.question: What is WBGT?
faq.answer: Wet Bulb Globe Temperature (WBGT) is the gold standard for measuring heat stress risk. Unlike heat index, it accounts for humidity, wind, sun angle, and cloud cover. OSHA uses WBGT to set outdoor work limits.

faq.question: At what temperature should outdoor work stop?
faq.answer: There's no single temperature. It depends on humidity, sun exposure, and work intensity. OSHA guidelines: rest breaks start at WBGT 77-86°F depending on workload. Above WBGT 90°F, most outdoor work should be limited to essential tasks.

faq.question: How can employers reduce heat stress risk?
faq.answer: Provide water every 15 minutes, shade breaks, gradual acclimatization (2 weeks), light-colored clothing, buddy system, and training on heat illness signs. OSHA requires a heat injury prevention program when temperatures exceed 80°F.
